The benchmark is operationalized as a visual question answering (VQA) task, with one source noting it contains 346 images and 1129 questions, while another mentions it evaluates across dimensions like question type and difficulty. While its main purpose is hallucination detection, a smaller number of papers also employ HallusionBench to evaluate broader capabilities such as "Visual question answering" and "Visual reasoning." In practice, it is frequently used in evaluation suites alongside other hallucination-focused instruments like POPE and CHAIR.

nodes.name: HaluEval
nodes.definition: HaluEval is consistently described across the provided literature as a large-scale benchmark for evaluating factual hallucination in model-generated responses. Its primary and most frequent application is to measure the construct of `Hallucination`, with several papers also using it to specifically assess `Hallucination detection`. One paper notes the benchmark is built from "synthetically generated hallucination data," while another specifies that its question-answering (QA) track provides a question, reference knowledge, a correct answer, and a hallucinated answer. The operationalization of HaluEval varies across studies, reflecting different facets of hallucination evaluation. A prevalent use is in QA tasks, where performance is measured by metrics such as "Accuracy", "Correctness", and "Exact Match". A different approach, reported by one study, uses it for a binary classification task to evaluate a model’s ability "to judge whether an answer to a question contains a hallucination or not." The benchmark is situated within the context of "factual reasoning" and is also used to validate `Generalization` in at least one paper.

nodes.name: HANNA
nodes.definition: Dataset for evaluating short stories generated from prompts, with human ratings on narrative qualities such as surprise and coherence.

nodes.name: HANS
nodes.definition: HANS is predominantly characterized as a dataset for Natural Language Inference (NLI), frequently described as a 'stress test' or 'adversarial' dataset. Across the provided papers, its main purpose is to evaluate a model's reliance on simple heuristics, with 'lexical overlap' being the most commonly cited heuristic it is designed to challenge. One paper elaborates that it manipulates features like subsequence and negation to expose reliance on 'syntactic heuristics rather than semantic understanding.' In practice, HANS is used to measure the behavior of `Natural language inference` and, in one instance, the construct of `Safety`. Researchers also use it for more specific assessments, such as evaluating 'minority generalization' and assessing 'length bias in binary classification.' The dataset, which one source notes contains 30K examples, is applied in both finetuning and in-context learning (ICL) evaluation settings. The consistent focus on testing against heuristics positions HANS as an instrument for evaluating whether models move beyond superficial cues for NLI tasks.

nodes.name: HardEN
nodes.definition: Challenging word sense disambiguation benchmark consisting of instances that were not correctly disambiguated by any system in prior evaluations, used to assess performance on difficult or out-of-distribution examples.

nodes.name: HarM
nodes.definition: Harmful Meme dataset focused on COVID-19-related memes, used to evaluate zero-shot detection of harmful content in a public health context.

nodes.name: Harmbench
nodes.definition: Across the surveyed literature, Harmbench is predominantly described as a benchmark of harmful instructions or behaviors used to evaluate the safety and robustness of large language models, particularly under adversarial or jailbreak attacks. Papers frequently use Harmbench to measure behaviors such as `Jailbreaking`, `Harmlessness`, `Safety`, and `Adversarial robustness`. It is also employed to assess `Harmful request refusal`, `Harmful content generation`, and the `Generalization` of attack methods. The benchmark is operationalized as a dataset of harmful prompts, with papers citing various sizes from 80 to 400 items; one source specifies a set of "200 standardized harmful behaviors" covering topics like cybercrime and misinformation. Evaluation of model responses is often performed using an associated classifier, with one paper identifying "HarmBench-Llama2-13B-Chat" for this role, while another notes the use of GPT-3.5-turbo to assess jailbreak success. While some definitions focus on specific attack types like "embedding-level SCAV attacks," a smaller line of work frames it as an "'out-of-distribution' test set" to evaluate attack generalization from other datasets. A variant

nodes.name: HarmBench classifier
nodes.definition: An LLM-as-a-judge safety classifier fine-tuned to assess whether model outputs are harmful.

nodes.name: Harmful instructions
nodes.definition: A safety benchmark used to evaluate VLM behavior under harmful multimodal jailbreak prompts.

nodes.name: Harry Potter dataset
nodes.definition: The name "Harry Potter dataset" and its variants, such as "BOOKS", are predominantly used in the provided literature to describe a measurement instrument for evaluating LLM unlearning of copyrighted content. The dataset is built from passages of the Harry Potter book series, with one paper specifying the use of "Harry Potter and the Sorcerer’s Stone". Its primary function is to test whether a model "stops reproducing the source text" after undergoing an unlearning process. One paper describes a specific operationalization within the MUSE benchmark where the original novels constitute the "forget set" and related FanWiki materials serve as the "retain set." This structure is intended to evaluate if the model can unlearn the copyrighted text while preserving related domain knowledge. A different line of work mentions a dataset also named "Books," which is an Amazon dataset of user-item interactions used for evaluating recommendation performance, indicating a potential naming overlap for distinct instruments.

nodes.name: HateCheck
nodes.definition: A benchmark dataset for evaluating hate speech detection models, used in this paper as a source of unsafe, hate-speech-related prompts for the LITMUS benchmark.

nodes.name: Hateful Memes
nodes.definition: Across the provided literature, Hateful Memes is consistently framed as a measurement instrument for evaluating models on multimodal content. It is defined as both a "multimodal meme classification benchmark" and a "dataset for detecting hate speech," with the central task being to determine if an "image-text combination is hateful." The evaluation requires classifying whether a meme, including its image and text caption, "describes hateful intentions" or "promotes hateful sentiments." Papers in this set use Hateful Memes to operationalize and measure several constructs. It is most directly used to assess `Hate speech detection` and is also employed to evaluate the broader construct of `Harmlessness`. One definition specifies that the task requires "reasoning beyond simple unimodal signals," which aligns with its use in the literature to measure `Multimodal reasoning`.

nodes.name: HatefulMemes
nodes.definition: Binary classification benchmark that evaluates whether a meme image with text contains harmful content, used to assess task mapping in specific-mapping scenarios.

nodes.name: HateXplain
nodes.definition: Across the provided literature, HateXplain is predominantly described as a natural-language benchmark for text classification. Its primary use is to classify sentences as "normal versus hateful or offensive," a task also referred to as "toxic text classification." One paper specifies that the target label is binary, with '0' for normal sentences and '1' for those deemed hateful or offensive ("Debiasing Attention Mechanism in Transformer without Demographics"). A recurring focus is its application in evaluating model fairness and robustness, particularly in relation to demographics. The dataset is characterized as having "unwanted correlation between toxicity labels and mentions of demographics" and "demographic correlation shifts," making it a tool for assessing fairness concerning "gender-related sensitive information" and for evaluating "zero-shot robustness." A different, less frequent usage frames HateXplain as a "subjective single-label dataset" employed to contextualize multi-label research findings ("REARANK: Reasoning Re-ranking Agent via Reinforcement Learning").

nodes.name: HC3
nodes.definition: The Human ChatGPT Comparison Corpus (HC3) is predominantly used as a benchmark for evaluating machine-generated text detection. Across the provided literature, it is consistently described as a dataset containing paired responses from both humans and ChatGPT (specifically GPT-3.5) to the same questions, often in a Q&A format. A frequently highlighted feature is its multiscale design, offering both full-text and sentence-level variants in English and Chinese to assess "multiscale detection performance." The short-text versions are specifically named "HC3-English-Sent and HC3-Chinese-Sent." While the prevailing application is for human-AI text discrimination tasks, such as training an encoder on its human-written texts, a less common use is also documented. One paper employs HC3 not for simple detection but to "validate... differences between ChatGPT and human responses" and compare their qualitative characteristics.

nodes.name: HealthCareMagic
nodes.definition: Medical consultation dataset containing single-turn patient queries paired with licensed professional responses, used for in-distribution evaluation of medical QA systems.

nodes.name: HellaSwag
nodes.definition: Across the surveyed literature, HellaSwag is a benchmark predominantly used to evaluate a model's commonsense capabilities. The task is consistently operationalized as a multiple-choice challenge where a model must select the most plausible or natural continuation for a given text snippet or scenario. Papers most frequently use HellaSwag to measure `commonsense reasoning` and `natural language understanding`, with some also using it to assess the broader construct of `reasoning`. The prevailing evaluation method is zero-shot, though a smaller line of work employs few-shot settings, such as 10-shot accuracy. While most papers frame it as a completion task, a few define it as a form of `natural language inference`. Beyond these core applications, the benchmark is also used to assess other model behaviors, including `generalization`, `language modeling`, and `contextual understanding`. Its administration is often facilitated by tools like the `LLM Evaluation Harness`, and it is included as a component of the `Open LLM Leaderboard`.

nodes.name: HelloBench
nodes.definition: A comprehensive, in-the-wild, and open-ended benchmark designed to evaluate the performance of large language models in generating long text.

nodes.name: HELM
nodes.definition: Across the provided literature, HELM is predominantly characterized as a large-scale, comprehensive benchmark for language models, with its full name being Holistic Evaluation of Language Models. Most sources describe it as a framework for evaluating models across a wide range of scenarios, metrics, capabilities, limitations, and risks. It is frequently listed alongside other evaluation suites such as MMLU, BigBench, and Open LLM Leaderboards. In terms of its application, one paper uses HELM to rank the performance of LLMs on the MMLU benchmark, and it is generally positioned as a "proxy measurement of the true capabilities of LLMs." A single paper presents a starkly different definition, describing HELM as a non-adaptive attack method for watermark evasion that involves adding typos or converting text to lowercase. The benchmark is also reported to be "strongly correlated" with LiveBench.

nodes.name: HELMET
nodes.definition: Across the provided literature, HELMET is consistently characterized as a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating the performance of large language models on long-context tasks. It is described as a composite instrument, with one paper noting that it "includes portions from popular long-text benchmarks such as RULER... and ∞Bench" (MorphMark: Flexible Adaptive Watermarking for Large Language Models). Papers use HELMET to assess a diverse set of capabilities, though the exact scope varies slightly across descriptions; one source states its evaluation spans five task types including synthetic recall, retrieval-augmented generation, and long-document QA, while another mentions it covers seven categories. Broadly, HELMET is used to measure constructs and behaviors such as long-context understanding, information retrieval, memorization, and length generalization. In terms of its operationalization, HELMET is reported to curate versions of existing datasets like HotpotQA, Natural Questions, and TriviaQA for its own long-context evaluations. Researchers commonly employ it in experiments to demonstrate enhancements in a model's long-context processing and memory abilities.

nodes.name: HelpSteer
nodes.definition: A dataset designed to align LLM outputs with human-preferred characteristics, containing samples with human-annotated attribute ratings for helpfulness, correctness, coherence, complexity, and verbosity.

nodes.name: HelpSteer2
nodes.definition: Across the provided literature, HelpSteer2 is most frequently described as a multi-aspect alignment dataset, though it is also referred to as a preference dataset and an instruction-following dataset. The dataset is employed in two primary ways: for training models through preference optimization and for developing personalized reward models, as well as for evaluating alignment. As an evaluation instrument, HelpSteer2 is used to measure personalized alignment, with one paper noting its validation split contains 1,000 prompts for assessing "alignment across different preference dimensions." It is also reported to measure the constructs of faithfulness, helpfulness, complexity, and verbosity. A distinct line of work uses HelpSteer2 as a source of 'real data' for supervised fine-tuning experiments to demonstrate and study the phenomenon of model collapse. The dataset is noted to contain prompts, completions, and metrics, including a helpfulness score. Finally, the dataset is studied in relation to ShareGPT and Dialogue.

nodes.name: Hendrycks MATH
nodes.definition: A benchmark dataset consisting of challenging mathematics problems used to evaluate mathematical reasoning capabilities.

nodes.name: HESEIA
nodes.definition: A Spanish-language dataset of 45,416 sentences created by high-school teachers and students in Latin America to evaluate social biases in LLMs, organized by school subject and annotated for intersectional demographic biases.

nodes.name: HEX-PHI
nodes.definition: Across the provided literature, HEX-PHI is consistently characterized as a safety benchmark or dataset composed of harmful instructions designed to evaluate language models. Several papers specify its composition, noting that it consists of 330 harmful queries or instructions distributed across 11 categories. The primary application of this benchmark is to evaluate a model's safety-related properties. It is most frequently used to measure a model's general 'safety' and 'safety alignment', with one paper stating its purpose is to "evaluate the safety alignment of a model" (SEAL: Safety-enhanced Aligned LLM Fine-tuning via Bilevel Data Selection). Other papers use it to assess 'harmlessness' and to evaluate model responses in the context of 'adversarial attacks'. The evaluation generally focuses on determining whether models "comply with harmful requests and produce unsafe outputs" (Safety Alignment Should be Made More Than Just a Few Tokens Deep). A smaller portion of the literature also highlights its use for assessing compliance with "privacy-harming and personally harmful instructions" (AfriMed-QA: A Pan-African, Multi-Specialty, Medical Question-Answering Benchmark Dataset).

nodes.name: HEXACO-60
nodes.definition: A 60-item personality inventory used to measure six major dimensions of personality: Honesty-Humility, Emotionality, Extraversion, Agreeableness, Conscientiousness, and Openness to Experience.

nodes.name: HiTab
nodes.definition: HiTab is consistently described across the provided literature as a benchmark dataset for evaluating model performance on complex tables. The dataset is composed of tables sourced from government reports and Wikipedia pages, with one paper specifying it contains 3,597 tables and 10,672 question-answering pairs. The prevailing framing is that HiTab's primary challenge lies in its focus on "hierarchical tables," requiring models to handle "multi-level headers," "implicit relationships," and "complex structural relationships." In practice, papers use HiTab to measure several capabilities, including `Table understanding`, `Table and chart reasoning`, and `Text generation`. These capabilities are operationalized through tasks such as question answering and table transformation. For example, one study uses the benchmark to demonstrate that a new method "boosts QA accuracy" on the dataset. One paper also positions HiTab as a "widely used table reasoning dataset" alongside others in the field.

nodes.name: HotpotQA
nodes.definition: Across the provided literature, HotpotQA is consistently defined as a benchmark for question answering that requires multi-hop reasoning. The core task involves finding and integrating evidence from multiple supporting documents, which are frequently specified as originating from Wikipedia. Papers use HotpotQA to measure several capabilities, most prevalently multi-hop question answering, but also complex reasoning, information retrieval, and document question answering. The benchmark is applied in various settings, including open-domain, closed-book, and a "distractor setting" where irrelevant paragraphs are mixed with relevant ones. While most descriptions are consistent, there is some variation in how its outputs are characterized; one paper notes it involves "long answers containing many tokens," whereas another describes answers as "typically being short entities or yes/no responses." Its use extends beyond direct evaluation, as it is included within other benchmarks like LongBench and is sometimes used to generate synthetic training data or as a probing set for model analysis.

nodes.name: HoVer
nodes.definition: Multi-hop fact verification benchmark requiring evidence aggregation across multiple documents, based on Wikipedia, with claims structured in 2-hop, 3-hop, and 4-hop reasoning paths.

nodes.name: How2Sign
nodes.definition: Across the provided literature, How2Sign is consistently characterized as a large-scale dataset and benchmark for American Sign Language (ASL). Its most prevalent application is to measure the behavior of sign language translation, specifically from ASL to English. Multiple sources state that the "SLT task is conducted on... How2Sign," and it is described as a standard benchmark for this purpose. The dataset's content is specified as being collected from "instructional videos" or focusing on the domain of "'how to' instructional videos." One source provides more granular details, noting it includes "35,191 samples with a vocabulary of 16K English words." A less common application is in the context of sign language recognition, where one paper reports using the dataset's training set to finetune models for language identification tasks. How2Sign is frequently mentioned alongside other datasets such as CSL-Daily and PHOENIX14T.

nodes.name: HPS v2
nodes.definition: Human Preference Score v2, a reward model trained on the HPD v2 dataset, used as a state-of-the-art baseline for comparison.

nodes.name: HSSD
nodes.definition: A dataset of synthesized, single-floor indoor scenes used to create environments for the Habitat-MAS benchmark.

nodes.name: Hugging Face Open LLM Leaderboard
names: Hugging Face Open LLM Leaderboard v1
nodes.definition: The Hugging Face Open LLM Leaderboard is consistently characterized across the provided literature as a widely recognized public platform or evaluation suite for ranking and tracking the performance of open-source large language models. It is most commonly used to assess general reasoning and knowledge capabilities, with papers specifically employing it to measure constructs such as `commonsense knowledge`, `mathematical reasoning`, `faithfulness`, and `human preference alignment`. The leaderboard operationalizes these assessments by aggregating model performance across a standardized set of academic benchmarks, frequently including MMLU, ARC, HellaSwag, TruthfulQA, WinoGrande, and GSM8K. The evaluation methodology is often based on few-shot performance, and as one paper notes, it "primarily utiliz[es] accuracy as the evaluation metric" ("Benchmarking LLMs via Uncertainty Quantification"). Several sources state that the leaderboard is based upon the Eleuther AI Language Model Evaluation Harness. Beyond direct model evaluation, the platform also serves as a data source for secondary analyses, such as studying "similarity trends across capability levels" ("Great Models Think Alike an

nodes.name: HuggingFace Open LLM Leaderboard 2
nodes.definition: A public leaderboard and evaluation suite from HuggingFace used to assess instruction following and world knowledge.

nodes.name: Human annotation study
nodes.definition: A human evaluation protocol where 84 participants each analyzed and classified 18 news stories (9 real, 9 fake) as either real or fake without using the internet.

nodes.name: Human baseline
nodes.definition: A human performance benchmark for information updating, derived from independent annotators manually synchronizing tables, used to compare model outputs on a subset of 100 examples.

nodes.name: Human evaluation
nodes.definition: Across the surveyed literature, human evaluation represents a broad category of assessment protocols frequently positioned as the "gold standard" for judging model outputs, particularly for open-ended tasks. It is commonly used to validate and measure the correlation of automated metrics, with several papers noting that evaluations from an `LLM-as-a-judge` can align closely with human assessments. The most prevalent operationalizations involve human annotators performing pairwise comparisons—such as A/B testing or side-by-side evaluations—to select a preferred response, or rating outputs on multi-point scales according to predefined rubrics. Other common procedures include labeling outputs for specific attributes like safety, spot-checking automated grades, and establishing performance baselines by having humans complete the benchmark tasks themselves. Researchers use these methods to assess a wide range of qualities, most frequently targeting core alignment dimensions such as helpfulness, harmlessness, and faithfulness. It is also widely applied to measure linguistic quality (including fluency and coherence), instruction following, and the presence of object hallucination. The eva

nodes.name: Human subject study
nodes.definition: Across the surveyed literature, human subject studies represent a versatile category of measurement methods that involve human participants in evaluating AI systems or collecting data. One common application is for quality assurance, where participants are asked to assess the "reliability and quality" of generated test cases and model responses. These studies are also operationalized as a "data collection procedure" to establish human performance baselines, for example by having participants perform a task to compute "humans’ raw accuracy". A prevalent use involves deploying surveys to gather perspectives from specific populations, such as surveying community members to identify "harmful model behaviours" or understanding "adoption patterns, use cases, and challenges" among users with visual impairments. The methodologies described vary, including "mixed-methods survey[s]" with "open-ended questions and Likert scale ratings" and procedures where participants submit guesses after viewing a sequence of clues.

nodes.name: HumanEval
nodes.definition:
Across the provided literature, HumanEval is consistently characterized as a benchmark for evaluating code generation in large language models. The dominant use is to measure the functional correctness of generated Python code, which is operationalized by assessing whether a model's output passes a series of unit tests for a set of hand-crafted programming problems. The Pass@1 metric is frequently reported for this evaluation. Several sources describe the benchmark as containing 164 problems, and the task is often framed as function completion from a docstring. One paper notes it "has emerged as the standard for this task" ("OctoPack: Instruction Tuning Code Large Language Models"), while another calls it "the most widely used code generation benchmark" ("The Stochastic Parrot onLLM’s Shoulder: A Summative Assessment of Physical Concept Understanding").

Papers in this set most frequently use HumanEval to measure the behavior of `code generation` and the construct of `programming ability`, with some also using it to assess `algorithmic reasoning`. The base benchmark has also been adapted into several variants for more specialized evaluations. For instance, `HumanEval+` is a recurri

nodes.name: HumanEval-X
nodes.definition: HumanEval-X is consistently described across the provided literature as a multilingual benchmark for evaluating large language models on code-related tasks. The definitions converge on its role in assessing model-generated code, with one paper framing this as evaluating 'code generation capabilities' and another as assessing 'functional correctness' and 'model utility'. The benchmark is also referred to as 'HumanEval-Multi', described as the 'multi-lingual version of Humaneval' in one study. In practice, papers use HumanEval-X to measure several capabilities. The most frequently measured behavior is Text generation, though it is also used to assess Faithfulness and Machine translation. Its application extends to evaluating specific models like DeepSeekV2-Lite and serving as a general 'test dataset for utility'. One study also leverages the benchmark to analyze internal model mechanics, such as 'expert activation frequencies' ('MxMoE: Mixed-precision Quantization for MoE with Accuracy and Performance Co-Design').

nodes.name: HumanEvalPack
nodes.definition: HumanEvalPack is a code benchmark used here as a source of prompts for the code split.

nodes.name: HumanEvalPlus
nodes.definition: An enhanced version of the HumanEval benchmark that includes more comprehensive and rigorous unit tests to better assess the functional correctness of generated code.

nodes.name: Humanity’s Last Exam
nodes.definition: Humanity’s Last Exam (HLE) is a challenging benchmark consisting of difficult exam questions across various academic and professional domains.

nodes.name: HumanML3D
nodes.definition: HumanML3D benchmark of human motions and textual descriptions used for evaluating text-to-motion and motion-to-text performance.

nodes.name: Humor Style Questionnaire
nodes.definition: Standardized 32-item psychological assessment measuring four humor styles—affiliative, self-enhancing, aggressive, and self-defeating—using a 5-point Likert scale, used here to probe LLMs' representation of humor-related constructs.

nodes.name: HybridQA
nodes.definition: HybridQA is a question answering benchmark combining table evidence with textual passages for hybrid reasoning.

nodes.name: Hypersim
nodes.definition: A photorealistic synthetic dataset used as a transfer task to evaluate the model's fine-tuned performance on semantic segmentation.

nodes.name: HyPoradise
nodes.definition: A generative error correction benchmark for LLM-enhanced ASR containing over 316K hypotheses-transcription pairs from mainstream ASR corpora, used to evaluate H2T mapping.

nodes.name: ICL CIPHERS
nodes.definition: A cryptographic evaluation framework that applies bijective and non-bijective token-level substitution ciphers to in-context learning inputs to assess task learning by comparing performance gaps between reversible and irreversible mappings.

nodes.name: iCliniq
nodes.definition: Real-world medical consultation records from the iCliniq platform, used as an out-of-distribution test set to evaluate generalization in medical QA.

nodes.name: IconQA
nodes.definition: Across the provided literature, IconQA is consistently characterized as a dataset for visual question answering (VQA). The specific focus of this dataset is on 'diagram understanding', a task described as a form of VQA. In practice, IconQA is employed as a downstream dataset for fine-tuning Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs). It is used to evaluate visual reasoning capabilities and is sometimes studied alongside other VQA datasets such as ScienceQA. One source also identifies IconQA as a component task within the CMIT benchmark.

nodes.name: IEMOCAP
nodes.definition: Across the provided literature, IEMOCAP is consistently described as a benchmark database used to assess emotion-related tasks. The prevailing usage is for evaluating "emotion recognition from speech," a framing present in multiple sources. A related description characterizes it as a "multimodal dataset for emotion classification from spoken dialogue" and notes its application in evaluating "text-based emotion detection in long conversational sequences" (SUA: Stealthy Multimodal Large Language Model Unlearning Attack). In line with this, the dataset is used to measure the behavior of Classification. Researchers use IEMOCAP to report model performance, with one study noting accuracy improvements over other models. One paper specifies that the dataset contains 1,241 utterances and it is mentioned alongside other benchmarks like MELD and MultiWOZ 2.2.

nodes.name: IFEval
nodes.definition: Across the surveyed literature, IFEval is consistently defined as a targeted benchmark for evaluating the instruction-following capabilities of large language models. The predominant use of IFEval is to measure a model's ability to adhere to explicit and verifiable constraints given in a prompt, which are assessed through what one paper calls a "standardized and automated assessment methodology." These constraints are programmatically checkable, with examples cited including requirements to "'write more than 400 words'" or perform specific formatting tasks like "JSON formatting, capitalizing, highlighting text, lowercasing, creating bullet lists, and using quotations." Researchers frequently employ IFEval alongside other instruments like MMLU, GSM8K, and AlpacaEval to assess a model's general capabilities, and it is noted as being part of the TÜLU-2 evaluation suite. While its primary application is clear, some studies show variations in its use, such as adapting it for "rule-following tasks" or using it in both text-based and synthesized spoken forms. A few papers also position IFEval as a measure of "Helpfulness" or "Faithfulness." The provided data also indicates a relationship

nodes.name: iGSM
nodes.definition: A synthetic dataset of program-generated, elementary-school level math reasoning problems with step-by-step solutions, designed to be controllable and verifiable.

nodes.name: IIRC
nodes.definition: Across the provided sources, IIRC (Iterative Information Retrieval and Comprehension) is consistently characterized as a benchmark dataset for reading comprehension used for evaluation. The dataset is described as focusing on information integration, requiring models to answer questions by "linking information across document sections" to test both retrieval and contextual understanding. In practice, papers use IIRC to measure both `Question answering` and `Mathematical reasoning`. The measurement of mathematical reasoning is specifically associated with using "decontextualized versions of a subset of ... IIRC," as noted in one study. Its general application is for evaluation, with one paper reporting that a model "demonstrates a consistent advantage across metrics" on the dataset.

nodes.name: IIW-400
nodes.definition: A dataset consisting of 400 image-caption pairs with highly detailed, hallucination-free captions, used for evaluating caption quality.

nodes.name: ImageNet-100
nodes.definition: A subset of the ImageNet dataset used for evaluating image classification performance.

nodes.name: ImageNet-1k
nodes.definition: Across the surveyed literature, ImageNet-1k is predominantly characterized as a large-scale benchmark dataset used to measure `Image classification`. Most definitions describe it as containing over a million images organized into 1,000 object categories, and its most frequent application is for evaluating core classification accuracy, though it is also widely used for pre-training vision models. A smaller but notable line of work employs ImageNet-1k to evaluate `Image generation`, with one paper calling it a "gold standard benchmark" for this task and another specifying the use of FID and Inception Score for assessing quality. Beyond these uses, the dataset is also operationalized to measure a range of other behaviors, including `Zero-shot image classification`, `Multimodal alignment`, and `Object recognition`. The data shows varied applications, from using its validation set to construct typographic attacks to modifying its images into flattened patches to evaluate cross-modal compression. Some studies also use it to assess `Generalization` and `Multimodal understanding`. The provided data also defines related but distinct benchmarks, such as `ImageNet-A`, which is specifically us

nodes.name: ImageNet-C
nodes.definition: A standard procedure and dataset used to generate corrupted versions of images with perturbations like snow, brightness changes, and motion blur to test model robustness.

nodes.name: ImageNet-R
nodes.definition: Across the provided literature, ImageNet-R is consistently described as a benchmark for evaluating model performance. The most common framing positions it as a tool for assessing model robustness to out-of-distribution images. A related but more specific definition from one paper states it is designed to test generalization to novel styles. The benchmark's content is consistently characterized as consisting of various artistic renditions of ImageNet object classes. Examples provided in the source materials include 'paintings, cartoons, sculptures' and 'embroidery'.

nodes.name: ImageNet-Segmentation
nodes.definition: A dataset providing pixel-level segmentation masks for a subset of the ImageNet dataset, used for evaluating semantic segmentation and object localization.

nodes.name: ImageNet-Sketch
nodes.definition: Across the provided literature, ImageNet-Sketch is consistently described as a dataset or benchmark used to evaluate model performance on out-of-distribution (OOD) data. The definitions agree that it contains "sketch-like images," with one source specifying they are derived from ImageNet. Papers use this instrument to assess constructs like model generalization and model robustness. Specifically, it is used to measure performance on the task of image classification. The framing as an "out-of-distribution benchmark" is a common thread in its description, and its operationalization involves evaluating models on it alongside other image datasets, as one paper notes.

nodes.name: ImageNetV2
nodes.definition: Across the provided literature, ImageNetV2 is consistently characterized as a test set or benchmark derived from ImageNet. The papers offer slightly different but related framings of its purpose. One definition states it is used to test "robustness under natural distribution shift." Another paper describes it as a tool for measuring "generalization to new, similarly distributed data," specifying that it was collected with a replicated protocol. In terms of its application, it is used for model evaluations, sometimes in conjunction with other datasets. As one study notes, "We use the ImageNetV2, ImageNet-R, ImageNet-A, and ImageNetSketch datasets for these evaluations".

nodes.name: IMDb
nodes.definition: Across the surveyed literature, IMDb is consistently defined as a benchmark dataset of movie reviews used for binary sentiment classification. Its most frequent application is to measure and operationalize `Sentiment analysis` and general `Text classification`, where models must predict whether a review is positive or negative. Multiple sources describe it as a "large movie review dataset for binary classification". A distinct, recurring application uses IMDb to evaluate controlled text generation and what some papers term `sentiment alignment`. In these cases, the task is not classification but rather generating reviews with a specific sentiment, such as producing "positive comments on movies" to test alignment without human feedback. Less common uses found in the data include serving as an out-of-distribution target task and its use in recommendation system research. The dataset is also listed as a measure for `Adaptability`, `Generalization`, and `Sentiment control`, among other behaviors.

nodes.name: IMHI
nodes.definition: In-distribution dataset for MentaLLaMA consisting of approximately 105,000 instruction-response pairs for mental health analysis, including depression detection and wellness classification.

nodes.name: IMPLIRET
nodes.definition: A benchmark for evaluating retrieval and reasoning capabilities when relevance depends on implicit facts in documents, covering arithmetic, temporal, and world knowledge reasoning across uni- and multi-speaker discourse styles.

nodes.name: In-context attack
nodes.definition: A baseline evaluation setting that uses few-shot demonstrations of immoral or harmful behavior to induce the target model to endorse similar unethical behaviors.

nodes.name: INCLUDE
nodes.definition: INCLUDE, a native multilingual benchmark cited as culturally aware but lacking discipline balance and difficulty control.

nodes.name: IndoCulture
nodes.definition: An Indonesian benchmark used to assess downstream model performance after safety fine-tuning.

nodes.name: IndustryOR
nodes.definition: IndustryOR is characterized in the provided literature as an open-source dataset of commercial Operations Research problems. One paper specifies its composition as a benchmark of "100 problems spanning linear, integer, and mixed-integer programming at various difficulty levels." The dataset's application appears to vary across studies. For instance, one line of work uses IndustryOR as the basis for developing a new benchmark focused on explainable Operations Research. In contrast, another study employs it directly as a benchmark to measure the construct of `Faithfulness`. Thus, while its core content is consistently described as a collection of real-world optimization problems, it is used both as a foundational dataset and as a direct evaluation instrument.

nodes.name: InfiAgent-DABench
nodes.definition: A benchmark for data analysis containing 257 challenges with CSV inputs and multi-level (easy, medium, hard) analysis questions.

nodes.name: InfiniBench
nodes.definition: A large-scale benchmark for long-form video understanding in multi-modal models, assessing eight skills across 87.7K question-answer pairs from 1,000+ hours of movies and TV shows, using both multiple-choice and open-ended formats.

nodes.name: InfiniGram
nodes.definition: Search engine for efficient exact-match and near-verbatim retrieval of training data fragments across open pretraining corpora, used to verify whether generated sequences match known training content.

nodes.name: InfiniteBench
nodes.definition: InfiniteBench is consistently characterized as a benchmark for evaluating large language models on tasks involving very long contexts, with sources frequently specifying lengths exceeding 100,000 tokens. Across the provided literature, its primary application is to measure the construct of `Long-context processing` by assessing an LLM's ability to understand and reason over long dependencies. The most common operationalization focuses on a narrow set of tasks, particularly long-book question answering (`En.QA`), multiple-choice question answering (`En.MC`), and `Text summarization` (`En.Sum`). However, a smaller set of papers describes a more diverse collection of what one source calls "10 real-world and synthetic tasks." This broader scope includes the evaluation of `Information retrieval` (such as passkey and key-value retrieval), `Code debugging`, and `Mathematical reasoning`. The benchmark is also used to assess more general capabilities like `Length generalization` and `Long-context understanding`. Several papers refer to it as a "widely used" instrument for these purposes.

nodes.name: InfoBench
nodes.definition: A benchmark for instruction adherence that decomposes complex instructions into simpler sub-instructions for granular evaluation.

nodes.name: InfographicVQA
nodes.definition: InfoVQA (InfographicVQA) is a benchmark dataset for visual question answering on infographics.

nodes.name: InfoSeek
nodes.definition: InfoSeek is described in the provided literature as a visual question answering (VQA) dataset focused on evaluating knowledge-seeking abilities. The single available definition specifies that its questions require a two-step process: first identifying an entity in an image and then querying for a related fact about it. In practice, papers use InfoSeek to measure constructs such as `Generalization` and `Commonsense understanding`. One study situates it as a "popular KB-VR benchmark" (Knowledge-Based Visual Reasoning), employing it for evaluation alongside other instruments like A-OKVQA and OK-VQA ("FaithfulRAG: Fact-Level Conflict Modeling for Context-Faithful Retrieval-Augmented Generation"). This usage aligns with its core design of connecting visual content to external factual knowledge.

nodes.name: InfoVQA
nodes.definition: Across the provided literature, InfoVQA is consistently characterized as a benchmark dataset for information-based visual question answering on document images. The prevailing usage of the benchmark is to evaluate a model's ability to extract specific information from these images, with papers describing its focus as "information localization and extraction." It is also referred to as a "single-page document understanding dataset." Papers use InfoVQA to measure several capabilities, including natural language understanding, optical character recognition, and fine-grained visual understanding. Reinforcing this, some sources classify it among "OCR-extensive benchmarks" that "require fine-grained visual information." One paper notes that tasks like InfoVQA often necessitate "understanding only small portions of text within an image." In practice, InfoVQA is frequently grouped with other text-rich evaluation datasets such as DocVQA, ChartQA, and TextVQA.

nodes.name: InjecAgent
nodes.definition: A red-teaming framework that uses a large language model (GPT-4) to automatically generate adversarial injection prompts for attacking LLM agents.

nodes.name: InstructBLIP
nodes.definition: Multimodal large language model used as the VQA module to answer visual questions based on image content.

nodes.name: InstruSum
nodes.definition: A benchmark dataset for evaluating instruction-controllable summary generation, containing articles, summary requirements, and model outputs.

nodes.name: Integrated gradients
nodes.definition: Across the provided literature, Integrated Gradients is consistently defined as a gradient-based attribution method used to determine the importance of input features for a model's prediction. The common operational definition involves calculating this importance by integrating or accumulating gradients along a path from a baseline input to the actual input. It is frequently employed as a model-dependent interpretability tool, for example, to identify words that contribute most to a text classification. Several papers use it as a baseline for comparison, with one describing it as a "stronger baseline" (Towards Interpreting Visual Information Processing in Vision-Language Models) and others situating it alongside methods like Shapley values and attention weights. While most sources frame it as a general feature attribution technique, a single paper offers a distinct perspective, describing it as a "more accurate (though more computationally expensive) approximation of causal effects" (Sparse Feature Circuits: Discovering and Editing Interpretable Causal Graphs in Language Models).

nodes.name: Interchange intervention
nodes.definition: Based on the provided data, Interchange intervention is presented as a method rooted in causal inference and causality theory. The single source paper defines its specific application as evaluating the correspondence between two distinct levels of analysis: a high-level causal model of a dataset and the internal feature representations within a low-level neural network model. In this context, the method is used to analyze how well a neural network's internal learned features align with a pre-specified causal structure. The available materials do not provide information on its relationships with other constructs or its application in specific tasks.

nodes.name: IOI task
nodes.definition: The Indirect Object Identification task, used to evaluate model understanding of internal circuits by testing if a model can identify the indirect object in sentences with a known structure.

nodes.name: IPIP-NEO-300
nodes.definition: A 300-item personality questionnaire from the International Personality Item Pool used to measure the Big Five personality traits, used in this paper as a generalization target for models aligned on IPIP-NEO-120 data.

nodes.name: IRCoT
nodes.definition: IRCoT is a retrieval-and-reasoning QA approach evaluated as a baseline for complex question answering.

nodes.name: IsaacGym
nodes.definition: A GPU-accelerated physics simulation environment for reinforcement learning, used to evaluate performance on nine challenging robotic locomotion and manipulation tasks.

nodes.name: Isabelle
nodes.definition: An interactive theorem proving environment used here to formalize mathematical statements and mechanically verify formal solution sketches.

nodes.name: IU-Xray
nodes.definition: Across the provided sources, IU-Xray is consistently described as a dataset containing chest X-ray images paired with their associated medical or radiology reports. The prevailing usage of this dataset is to evaluate the task of medical report generation, a purpose cited across multiple papers and definitions. Some sources specify this task further as "English report generation" or "radiology report generation." A less common application, mentioned in a smaller subset of the literature, is using IU-Xray to evaluate medical visual question answering. In practice, it is frequently used alongside the MIMIC-CXR dataset for these evaluation purposes. One paper notes a specific operationalization, mentioning the "IU-Xray (Demner-Fushman et al., 2016) dataset containing 590 test samples."

nodes.name: IWSLT2017
nodes.definition: International Workshop on Spoken Language Translation benchmark used to evaluate translation performance, particularly for low-resource language pairs.

nodes.name: IWSLT 2017
nodes.definition: Across the provided literature, IWSLT 2017 is consistently described as a benchmark dataset used to evaluate machine translation capabilities. The definitions commonly specify its origin as the International Workshop on Spoken Language Translation 2017, with one paper noting its content consists of "parallel documents sourced from TED talks" ("DelTA: An Online Document-Level Translation Agent Based on Multi-Level Memory"). Its most prevalent application is to measure the behavior of `Machine translation`. While this is the dominant use case, a smaller set of studies employs the dataset for more specific investigations. For example, one paper uses it for "zero-shot prompting experiments" ("Uncertainty-Aware Decoding with Minimum Bayes Risk"). Another study utilizes it in the context of text translation to study correlations between model self-evaluation and established metrics such as BLEU, ROUGE, and BERTScore.

nodes.name: JailbreakBench
nodes.definition: Across the provided literature, JailbreakBench is consistently defined as a standardized benchmark used to evaluate large language models. Its primary application is to measure a model's `Safety` and `Adversarial robustness` against jailbreak attacks, with papers also using it to assess `Harmlessness` and `Refusal behavior`. The benchmark is composed of harmful queries or instructions designed to test a model's resistance to generating disallowed content, and is listed in one paper among other "commonly used harmful query datasets" ("On the Role of Attention Heads in Large Language Model Safety"). In practice, researchers report using subsets of its data, such as sampling 100 harmful prompts for evaluation. A specific feature highlighted by one study is its evaluation protocol, which "recommends using LLM as a judge" to automatically assess whether a model produces a disallowed response ("FineReason: Evaluating and ImprovingLLMs’ Deliberate Reasoning through Reflective Puzzle Solving"). One paper also distinguishes between "template" and "direct" versions of the benchmark, though without further detail. The overall goal is to test "jailbreak resistance and safety degradation," part

nodes.name: JailbreakV
nodes.definition: A multimodal safety dataset used to evaluate jailbreak resistance and safety performance, identified in the paper as suffering from visual safety information leakage.

nodes.name: JAMA Clinical Challenge
nodes.definition: A dataset of complex clinical cases from the Journal of the American Medical Association, used to evaluate clinical decision-making in realistic scenarios with multiple-choice questions and expert explanations.

nodes.name: JEEBench
nodes.definition: Across the provided literature, JEEBench is consistently characterized as a benchmark dataset of mathematics problems used for evaluating large language models. Its primary stated purpose is to measure the construct of "Mathematical reasoning," with one paper describing its use for "evaluating mathematical reasoning on exam-style problems" (Omni-MATH: A Universal Olympiad Level Mathematic Benchmark for Large Language Models). The problems are consistently framed as "challenging." The content is described as covering "college-level mathematics topics," with one source specifying this includes "simple ODEs and multivariable calculus" (HARDMath: A Benchmark Dataset for Challenging Problems in Applied Mathematics). The benchmark is presented as one of several more difficult datasets proposed as language models have become more capable.

nodes.name: Jensen-Shannon divergence
nodes.definition: Soft evaluation metric used to measure the alignment between the distribution of model predictions and human annotators' label distributions across demographic traits.

nodes.name: Jigsaw
nodes.definition: A toxicity classification dataset where each sample is a sentence and sensitive attribute value is identified by humans.

nodes.name: JudgeBench
nodes.definition: Across the provided literature, JudgeBench is consistently characterized as a benchmark for evaluating LLM-as-a-Judge systems. The most prevalent usage is to assess these systems using what one paper describes as "challenging response pairs spanning knowledge, reasoning, math, and coding." In this capacity, JudgeBench is used to measure constructs including Mathematical reasoning, Commonsense knowledge, and Faithfulness. The benchmark's content is specified as being derived from other datasets, namely MMLU-Pro for its knowledge category, LiveBench for reasoning and mathematics, and LiveCodeBench for coding. Some papers use it to test the "robustness and accuracy" of judge models, while a less common framing positions it as a case study for evaluating "judge behavior and agreement under uncertainty." In the context of other evaluation tools, it is listed alongside instruments like RewardBench and MTBench-Human, and is explicitly described as a "valuable complement to RewardBench for evaluating reward models on difficult tasks requiring reasoning."

nodes.name: Just-Eval
nodes.definition: Across the provided literature, Just-Eval is described as an evaluation framework for assessing the outputs of large language models. It is consistently used to measure the construct of `Helpfulness`. The evaluation is multi-dimensional, with all sources noting that it assesses outputs along the aspects of helpfulness, clarity, factuality, depth, and engagement. As detailed in one paper, the framework is operationalized by using GPT-4 as a judge to score model outputs. This process involves scoring the output on a scale from 1 to 5 for each dimension, guided by what is referred to as the "official prompt" ("AfriMed-QA: A Pan-African, Multi-Specialty, Medical Question-Answering Benchmark Dataset").

nodes.name: K-means clustering
nodes.definition: Across the provided sources, K-means clustering is described as a statistical procedure used to group data points based on similarity. The prevailing application is to examine whether items form distinct groups in a high-dimensional space. For example, one paper uses K-means clustering on "LLM preference vectors" to group languages and identify "clusters of behavioral patterns." A different application involves using the method on "prompt embeddings" to see if they form natural groupings. This work mentions employing K-means clustering as one of two analytical methods, alongside "quantifying token importance." The procedure is implemented by running the algorithm with a pre-specified number of centers.

nodes.name: K-QA
nodes.definition: A real-world medical question-answering benchmark, with a subset of responses verified by physicians.

nodes.name: KATE
nodes.definition: An in-context learning evaluation framework for NER that selects demonstrations by performing a nearest neighbor search using sentence-level embeddings.

nodes.name: Keirsey Temperament Sorter
nodes.definition: A self-assessment personality questionnaire, based on the Myers-Briggs Type Indicator, used to classify individuals into one of four temperament groups: Rationals, Idealists, Guardians, and Artisans.

nodes.name: Keyword matching
nodes.definition: A rule-based evaluation strategy that checks for the presence of specific refusal-related keywords in a model's response, used as a baseline evaluator.

nodes.name: Kitchen
nodes.definition: A simulated benchmark (Gupta et al., 2019; Fu et al., 2020) designed to test an agent's ability to handle sparse terminal rewards and perform long-horizon manipulation in a kitchen setting.

nodes.name: KnowEdit
nodes.definition: Based on the provided data, KnowEdit is characterized as an integrated benchmark designed for evaluating knowledge editing methods. The benchmark is used to assess these methods against a set of specified criteria, which include success, portability, locality, and fluency. As described in one source, KnowEdit is composed of six distinct datasets, each corresponding to different types of evaluation. The available literature shows it being applied in experimental settings to test various knowledge editing approaches.

nodes.name: Knowledge distillation
nodes.definition: A model alignment technique where a public model is fine-tuned to mimic the output distributions of a private model using a dataset of prompts and corresponding private model outputs.

nodes.name: Kolmogorov-Smirnov test
nodes.definition: A statistical test used to measure distributional similarity between the unlearned model and a reference model for Forget Quality.

nodes.name: Konwinski Prize
nodes.definition: A benchmark designed to fairly evaluate state-of-the-art LLMs by using only new GitHub issues, thereby mitigating data contamination.

nodes.name: L-Eval
nodes.definition: Across the provided literature, L-Eval is predominantly characterized as a benchmark designed to assess the long-context capabilities of large language models. Papers use L-Eval to measure several related constructs, most commonly `Long-context processing` and `Long-context understanding`, with one paper also using it to evaluate `Long-form text generation`. The benchmark is consistently described as an evaluation suite composed of closed-ended tasks with varying input lengths, specifically multiple-choice and true/false question answering. One study specifies that its evaluation uses four particular closed-ended tasks from L-Eval: TOFEL, QuALITY, Coursera, and SFiction. In practice, L-Eval is often studied alongside other long-context benchmarks, including LongBench and LooGLE. A less common framing, found in one paper, describes L-Eval as a benchmark for "long-text and reasoning-oriented evaluations" used for comparative purposes with another benchmark, CS-Bench. The prevailing usage, therefore, positions L-Eval as an instrument for evaluating how LLMs handle long contexts, operationalized through a set of closed-ended question-answering tasks.

nodes.name: LAMBADA
nodes.definition: Across the provided literature, LAMBADA is predominantly characterized as a language modeling benchmark designed to evaluate a model's ability to predict the final word of a passage. This task is consistently described as requiring an understanding of broad discourse context and is frequently used to assess a model's capacity to handle long-range dependencies; as one paper states, it is a "last word completion task... to test how models capture long-range dependencies in text." The evaluation is commonly conducted in a zero-shot setting, with accuracy and perplexity being the reported metrics. While its most prevalent application is to measure `Language modeling`, papers also use LAMBADA to assess related constructs such as `Contextual understanding` and `Natural language understanding`. A smaller set of studies frames it as a `Reading comprehension` benchmark, and it is often included in larger evaluation suites to measure general `Downstream task performance` or `Text generation`.

nodes.name: Lambada-OpenAI
nodes.definition: Lambada-OpenAI is presented in the provided literature as a benchmark for evaluating language model capabilities. Its most common application is to measure `Language understanding`, a use case supported by multiple papers and its formal definition. A less frequent use is to assess `Commonsense knowledge`, as mentioned in one study. The benchmark is specifically defined as a text completion task designed to test a model's ability to handle long-range dependencies in text. One paper also notes its application in a zero-shot evaluation setting.

nodes.name: LaMP
nodes.definition: Across the provided literature, LaMP is consistently described as a public benchmark designed to evaluate LLM personalization. The benchmark is specifically positioned to assess short-form personalized text generation, with one paper noting it tests "surface-level personalization on short-context queries" ('Case-Based Decision-Theoretic Decoding with Quality Memories'). This focus on brevity is reinforced in another study that contrasts LaMP with the LongLaMP benchmark, which is used for "long-form personalized text generation" ('SongComposer: A Large Language Model for Lyric and Melody Generation in Song Composition'). The tasks included within the LaMP benchmark are varied, with sources collectively mentioning news headline generation, scholarly title generation, tweet paraphrasing, citation identification, movie tagging, and Amazon product rating. One paper explicitly enumerates these as distinct, numbered components, such as LaMP-1 for citation identification and LaMP-4 for news headline generation.

nodes.name: LaMP-QA
nodes.definition: A benchmark for evaluating personalized long-form question answering, covering questions from Arts & Entertainment, Lifestyle & Personal Development, and Society & Culture, using user profiles and aspect-based evaluation derived from question narratives.

nodes.name: Lap14
nodes.definition: A dataset for aspect-based sentiment analysis from the SemEval 2014 shared task, requiring classification of sentiment towards a particular entity or aspect.

nodes.name: LASER
nodes.definition: An LLM-based ASR evaluation rubric that uses detailed prompts with error type examples to assign no, minor, or major penalties based on semantic impact, producing a sentence-level score.

nodes.name: Last letter concatenation
nodes.definition: Across the provided literature, Last letter concatenation is consistently framed as a benchmark task used to evaluate different forms of reasoning in language models. The task itself is a form of string manipulation where a model must "concatenate the last letters of words in a given name," as exemplified by one paper's instance of transforming "Amy Brown" into "yn". Its most prevalent application is to measure `Symbolic reasoning`, a use case supported by multiple definitions and a relationship cited across five papers. Some work also uses it to evaluate "algorithmic reasoning" and "length generalization." A less common application, found in one paper, is its use to measure `Commonsense knowledge`. The benchmark is described as both "frequently used" and a "difficult string manipulation task," with performance typically reported as accuracy on the test set.

nodes.name: LastFM
nodes.definition: A benchmark dataset for recommendation research derived from a popular music service, containing user-artist interaction histories.

nodes.name: LawBench
nodes.definition: Based on the provided data, LawBench is a benchmark used to evaluate the generalization capability of LLMs in the legal domain. It is specifically characterized as focusing on Chinese laws. The instrument is described as containing 20 diverse legal tasks, with examples including charge prediction and prison term prediction. In the single paper provided, it is mentioned alongside LegalBench as a tool for assessing performance across a wide range of legal areas. All available information on this benchmark comes from one source paper, which defines its scope and application.

nodes.name: Layer-wise probing
nodes.definition: An analysis method that assesses the contribution of each individual model layer to prediction accuracy, revealing how task-specific knowledge is distributed across the model's architecture.

nodes.name: LeanDojo
nodes.definition: LeanDojo is a Lean theorem-proving benchmark and data-extraction ecosystem used as a comparison point for premise-based theorem proving.

nodes.name: LeetCode
nodes.definition: A platform providing a collection of coding problems, used as a source for creating fill-in-the-blank tasks.

nodes.name: LeetCode Contest
nodes.definition: A benchmark consisting of programming contest problems categorized by difficulty (Easy, Medium, Hard) to assess performance on complex problem-solving.

nodes.name: LegalBench
nodes.definition: Across the provided literature, LegalBench is consistently characterized as a measurement instrument for evaluating language models in the legal domain. The prevailing usage, as described in multiple definitions and relationships, is to assess the construct of legal reasoning. One paper specifies its composition, noting that "LegalBench consists of 162 tasks that evaluate various aspects of legal reasoning" ("TracSum: A New Benchmark for Aspect-Based Summarization with Sentence-Level Traceability in Medical Domain"). A less frequent framing also describes it as a dataset for evaluating hallucination and domain-specific factual accuracy in legal contexts. In practice, researchers use the benchmark for "cross-evaluations" and to "test models," operationalizing the assessment with metrics like balanced accuracy.

nodes.name: LEMON
nodes.definition: LEMON is presented in the provided literature as a measurement instrument for Chinese Spelling Correction (CSC). It is consistently defined as a large-scale, multi-domain dataset featuring real-world spelling errors across seven specified sub-domains: game, encyclopedia, contract, medical care, car, novel, and news. The most prevalent use of LEMON, as indicated by the relationships, is to operationalize and measure model generalization. Specifically, papers use it to assess cross-domain generalization, with one source stating it "serves as a cross-domain dataset for evaluating the generalization capabilities." Another source notes its utility for this purpose stems from containing "longer and more complex sentences." One paper also uses it for "Evaluation" more broadly. The dataset is reportedly used to test a model's "domain correction capabilities in a zero-shot setting."

nodes.name: LexEval
nodes.definition: Legal domain evaluation suite containing Legal Translation and Legal EssayQA tasks, assessing bilingual translation and open-ended legal reasoning.

nodes.name: LFQA
nodes.definition: A long-form question-answering dataset created by scraping Reddit questions and pairing them with human-written answers, used for evaluating watermark robustness in QA settings.

nodes.name: LIAR
nodes.definition: Based on the provided data, LIAR is a dataset used for evaluating claim verification. The single source paper, "MultiSocial: Multilingual Benchmark of Machine-Generated Text Detection of Social-Media Texts", operationalizes this task as true/false classification. Performance on the dataset is assessed using the F1 score, with the paper noting an instance of "improving F1 score by 10.1 on LIAR dataset". This source presents LIAR as one of several "standard datasets" used for evaluation.

nodes.name: LIAR2
nodes.definition: Benchmark dataset of approximately 23,000 short statements manually labeled by professional fact-checkers for truthfulness, used for fake news detection evaluation.

nodes.name: LibriSpeech
nodes.definition: Across the provided literature, LibriSpeech is consistently characterized as a large-scale corpus of read English speech, frequently noted to be derived from audiobooks from the LibriVox project and containing approximately 960 to 1000 hours of data. The most common application of this dataset is to measure `Speech recognition`, with multiple papers using it to evaluate Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) performance on its various test sets. Beyond ASR, LibriSpeech is also used to assess other capabilities, including `Data reconstruction`, phone recognition, and, in one instance, `Faithfulness`. Researchers operationalize the dataset by employing specific subsets for distinct purposes, such as using the "960-hour training set" for pre-training and various "dev" and "test" sets like "dev-clean" and "test-other" for evaluation. The data is also used for training tokenizers, estimating mutual information, and as prompts for speech continuation tasks, as noted in "A Variational Framework for Improving Naturalness in Generative Spoken Language Models". While some definitions frame it broadly as a corpus for tasks like compression, the prevailing usage in this set of papers is as a bench

nodes.name: LIMA
nodes.definition: Across the provided literature, LIMA is a natural language dataset with two distinct applications: model evaluation and model training. The most common use described in this set of papers is for evaluation, where it serves as an instrument to measure specific constructs. For instance, LIMA is used to measure `Memorization`, with one study employing it to "test the generalizability of findings" in a non-code domain. Another paper reports using LIMA to measure `Faithfulness`. A different line of work uses LIMA as a training resource. In this context, it is described as a dataset for "instruction fine-tuning" and is used for "additional training" of models, characterized as consisting of "curated prompts and responses designed to teach models to follow specific instructional formats."

nodes.name: LIME
nodes.definition: Based on the available data, LIME (Local Interpretable Model-agnostic Explanations) is characterized as a feature attribution technique used to explain the individual predictions of a model. The provided source describes it as a "widely-used off-the-shelf" method that is "frequently applied within specific NLP applications." This framing suggests its common use as a practical tool for model interpretation. As an example of its application, one study is cited for using LIME to elucidate hate speech detection models, particularly in the context of low-resource languages like Urdu and Sindhi.

nodes.name: Linear CKA
nodes.definition: Linear Centered Kernel Alignment (CKA) is a method for measuring the similarity between neural network representations, which the paper considered as an alternative analysis tool.

nodes.name: Linear Predictivity
nodes.definition: A brain alignment measurement procedure where a linear regression model is trained to predict brain activity from model representations, with performance measured by the Pearson correlation on held-out data.

nodes.name: Linear probe
nodes.definition: A linear probe is characterized as a method for assessing constructs by training a linear classifier on a large language model's internal hidden states. The available data specifies that Multilayer Perceptron (MLP) activations are used as inputs for the classifier. This technique is operationalized to predict latent constructs from these internal representations. For example, one study uses linear probes to measure "Semantic understanding," while another applies them to predict "trait impressions" like warmth and competence. As one paper states, "We train linear probes to predict trait impressions from ... LLM hidden representations" (LLM-Driven Completeness and Consistency Evaluation for Cultural Heritage Data Augmentation in Cross-Modal Retrieval). The effectiveness of this measurement approach is evaluated using classification metrics, with one study reporting that its probes' F1 scores surpassed a bag-of-words baseline.

nodes.name: LINGOLY
nodes.definition: Benchmark consisting of linguistic puzzles from the United Kingdom Linguistics Olympiad (UKLO), covering six topics: phonology, morphology, syntax, semantics, number systems, and compounding, used to evaluate LLM performance on puzzle solving.

nodes.name: LiRA
nodes.definition: Likelihood Ratio Attack, a membership inference attack that formulates the problem as hypothesis testing, comparing the likelihood of a model's output score under the hypotheses that the sample was or was not in the training data.

nodes.name: List functions
nodes.definition: Across the surveyed literature, List Functions is consistently presented as a dataset or benchmark designed to evaluate reasoning capabilities in both humans and machines. The core task requires identifying or inducing a function that transforms an input list into a specified output list, with one paper noting the lists are of numbers. The most prevalent application of this instrument is to measure `Inductive reasoning`, a connection explicitly made across multiple papers. Several sources also describe its purpose as evaluating 'concept learning'. As one study notes, it "evaluates an LLM’s ability to induce rules that transform input lists into output lists" (LLMs Can Plan Only If We Tell Them). A less common framing, found in one paper, characterizes it as a 'Programming by Example (PBE) benchmark task'. The dataset is described as 'cognitive-science-inspired' and was originally designed to investigate how humans learn computable functions.

nodes.name: LITRANSPROQA
nodes.definition: An LLM-based question-answering framework for literary translation evaluation that uses professionally curated questions to assess translation quality across multiple dimensions including literary devices, cultural understanding, and authorial voice.

nodes.name: LiveBench
nodes.definition: Across the provided literature, LiveBench is characterized as a dynamic benchmark designed to mitigate data contamination by regularly updating its dataset with questions from recent sources. Several papers specify its method, noting it "collects questions based on the latest information source, e.g., math competitions from the past 12 months, with new questions added and updated every few months" (Reshaping Representation Space to Balance the Safety and Over-rejection in Large Audio Language Models). The benchmark is consistently defined as an evaluation from Abacus AI that uses a large test set of questions with ground truth answers. Papers use LiveBench to measure a wide range of model capabilities, including mathematical reasoning, programming ability, data analysis, natural language understanding, commonsense knowledge, faithfulness, and instruction following. It is studied alongside other evaluation tools and is reported to be "strongly correlated" with HELM (Style Outweighs Substance: Failure Modes of LLM Judges in Alignment Benchmarking). Additionally, its reasoning and mathematics datasets are used by JudgeBench, and it is shown to correlate with LMSYS Chatbot Arena and Ar

nodes.name: LiveCodeBench
nodes.definition: Across the provided literature, LiveCodeBench is consistently described as a benchmark for evaluating large language models, with a frequently mentioned feature being its 'contamination-free' design. Its predominant use is to measure the `code generation` and general `programming ability` of models. The benchmark is composed of competitive programming problems, which one paper notes require "advanced reasoning capabilities," sourced from platforms like LeetCode, AtCoder, and CodeForces. To avoid data contamination, the benchmark employs a strategy of "careful segregation by date" and, as one source describes, "continuously collects new human-authored programming problems... to maintain freshness." While its primary application is assessing code generation, a smaller number of papers also use it to evaluate more specific skills like `abductive reasoning` and `code debugging`. A specific version, `LiveCodeBench-v1.0`, is mentioned as having an 'easy' subset for efficiency and accuracy comparisons. One paper also notes that the evaluation is execution-based.

nodes.name: LiveSum
nodes.definition: A synthetic football game comments dataset designed for text-to-table tasks, featuring multi-instance and dynamic data where team statistics are aggregated from individual player scores mentioned in the text.

nodes.name: LIWC
nodes.definition: Based on the provided data, LIWC (Linguistic Inquiry and Word Count) is characterized as a psycholinguistic feature extraction tool. The single available definition states that it operates by categorizing words into psychological and linguistic dimensions, with examples including emotion, cognition, and formality. One source snippet reinforces this, noting that the tool "provides psycholinguistic characteristics." In terms of its application, the data shows that LIWC is used to measure the construct of "Stylistic variation." It is also mentioned as a tool for generating features for segment comparison, sometimes alongside other instruments like WritePrint.

nodes.name: LIWC-22
nodes.definition: Linguistic Inquiry and Word Count 22, a text analysis program that calculates the percentage of words in a given text that fall into different linguistic and psychological categories.

nodes.name: Llama-Guard
nodes.definition: Across the provided literature, Llama-Guard is consistently described as a model used for safety evaluation. It is defined as a "dedicated safety classifier LLM" trained to detect unsafe content, and it is applied to both prompts and generated responses. Papers use Llama-Guard to operationalize safety assessment in a few ways: one study uses it to "determine the safety of the responses" from another model, while another employs it to evaluate "bypass rates," which are defined as the percentage of malicious requests it misclassifies as safe. In addition to its role as an evaluation tool, one paper also frames Llama-Guard as a deployable "safety mechanism" designed to "detect and filter adversarial or harmful prompts" in settings like multi-agent systems. The effectiveness of Llama-Guard itself is also a subject of study, with one paper noting that it can "fail to defend against" certain attacks.

nodes.name: Llama Guard 2
nodes.definition: Across the provided literature, Llama Guard 2 is predominantly characterized as an automated safety evaluation tool, frequently employed as an "LLM-as-a-judge." It is consistently defined as a safety classifier fine-tuned from Llama-3-8B. The instrument is used to measure constructs such as `Safety` and the behavior of `Harmful content generation`. Papers operationalize this by having Llama Guard 2 classify the harmfulness, toxicity, or safety of various outputs, including model responses, conversations, and documents. This classification is then used to compute metrics like Attack Success Rate, as noted in one study, or the percentage of unsafe responses. Its application appears in contexts like safety red-teaming evaluations, data filtering, and alignment training, where it is sometimes used alongside other evaluators like GPT-4. A less common application mentioned in one paper is using its feature layer as a source for semantic embeddings.

nodes.name: Llama Guard 3
nodes.definition: Across the provided literature, Llama Guard 3 is predominantly characterized as an automated tool for evaluating the safety of language model outputs, frequently described as a "safety classifier," "automated judge," or "content safety classification LLM." Its primary documented use is to measure `Harmful content generation` by classifying model responses as "harmful or safe." Papers specify that it is capable of identifying various types of harmful content, such as "violence, self-harm, illegal activities, and inappropriate sexual content." It is operationalized as a "discriminator in attack evaluation" and as an "auxiliary detection tool," with one source noting the "close alignment of Llama-guard-3 and human evaluations" ("Measuring memorization in language models via probabilistic extraction"). While most sources focus on this safety classification role, a few papers describe more specific or distinct applications. For example, one study employs it to "evaluate the fairness of model outputs," and another uses it to assess safety by measuring a model's "refusal rate to harmful queries." A different framing appears in one paper that characterizes Llama Guard 3 not just as a post-

nodes.name: LlamaGuard
nodes.definition: Across the provided literature, LlamaGuard is predominantly characterized as a model-based evaluation tool or instrument used to assess the safety of large language model outputs. It is most commonly described as a classifier that determines whether a response is harmful or safe, and is used to calculate metrics such as Attack Success Rate (ASR) and safety rates. Papers use LlamaGuard to operationalize and measure constructs such as Harmlessness and, to a lesser extent, Jailbreak resistance. Beyond a post-hoc evaluation tool, some work also frames it as a pretrained 'guardrail system' for content moderation or as a benchmark itself.
